definition: Astrology is divination by the stars and planets — reading celestial positions for their influence on earthly life. For most of history it was inseparable from astronomy.
history: Greek *astron*, 'star,' + *-logia*, 'study.' From Babylonian omen-astronomy through Ptolemy's *Tetrabiblos* (2nd c.) and the great Arabic astrologers, astrology was a rigorous technical science — it chose the hour to make a talisman or crown a king. Only in the 17th century did astrology and astronomy divorce; Kepler cast horoscopes to the end. RenaissanceMagicDB catalogues its apparatus of aspects, dignities, and houses.
commentary: Astrology sits at the center of the field's biggest methodological quarrel: whether it belongs to the history of *science* or the history of *superstition*. Lynn Thorndike's monumental *History of Magic and Experimental Science* refused the tidy story of reason shedding astral nonsense, treating prediction and natural inquiry as intertwined. Later scholars — Nicolas Weill-Parot, David Pingree, and the Warburg-descended tradition Paola Zambelli documents — sharpened the distinctions Thorndike blurred, separating astral image theory, mathematical practice, and judicial prognostication. The field now stresses that astrology was a rigorous technical discipline whose divorce from astronomy was late and contingent, not a failure waiting to be corrected.

commentary_sources.label: Lynn Thorndike, A History of Magic and Experimental Science
Nicolas Weill-Parot, on astrology and occult knowledge
Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic from the Medieval Latin and Islamic World to Renaissance Europe

etymology.note: Astrology and astronomy were a single science until the 17th century pried them apart; Kepler cast horoscopes for a living.
etymology.hook: When astrology and astronomy filed for divorce.

"Well, that's another night of research spoiled."
—Claire, batfolk <mark>astrologer</mark>
